plants.identification: Round fruit, red or green skin, white flesh with brown seeds.
preparation: Check fruit is fully ripe — firm but not hard.
Wash skin thoroughly with clean water.
Eat raw or cook into sauce. Remove seeds and core.
Do not eat seeds — they contain trace cyanide compounds.
plants.nutrition: Rich in fibre, Vitamin C, and antioxidants.
warnings: Apple seeds contain amygdalin — do not eat in large quantities.

plants.identification: Pear-shaped, dark green to black bumpy skin, large brown seed inside.
preparation: Check ripeness — soft when gently squeezed.
Cut lengthwise around the seed and twist to open.
Remove seed with a spoon.
Scoop out or slice the green flesh and consume.
plants.nutrition: High in healthy fats, potassium, Vitamin K and folate.
warnings: Skin and seed are not edible.
Toxic to many animals.

plants.identification: Elongated yellow fruit in clusters, distinctive curved shape.
preparation: Choose yellow or slightly spotted bananas for best sweetness.
Peel back the skin from the stem end.
Eat the flesh raw or cook — roast, boil or fry unripe ones.
plants.nutrition: High in potassium, Vitamin B6, and quick-release energy.
warnings: Wild bananas may contain large seeds — spit them out.

plants.identification: Small round blue-purple berries in clusters. Crown-like dimple at top.
preparation: Pick berries that are deep blue-purple and come off easily.
Rinse with clean water.
Eat raw by the handful or cook into jam or sauce.
plants.nutrition: Very high in antioxidants, Vitamin C and K.
warnings: Do not confuse with toxic nightshade berries — blueberries have a star-shaped crown on top.

plants.identification: Small round red to dark purple fruit hanging in pairs on long stalks.
preparation: Pick fully red or dark purple cherries — avoid green ones.
Wash thoroughly.
Eat flesh raw. Always remove and discard the stone (seed).
Do not crack open or chew the stone.
plants.nutrition: Contains Vitamin C, potassium, and anti-inflammatory compounds.
warnings: Cherry pits contain cyanogenic glycosides — never crush or eat the seed.

plants.identification: Small round green or purple berries growing in hanging clusters on vines.
preparation: Pick clusters where most berries are fully coloured.
Wash thoroughly.
Eat raw — seeds are edible but bitter.
Can be dried into raisins or boiled into juice.
plants.nutrition: Contains resveratrol, Vitamin C, and natural sugars.
warnings: Ensure it is not moonseed vine — moonseed has a crescent-shaped seed, grapes have round seeds.

plants.identification: Oval brown fuzzy skin, bright green or yellow flesh with tiny black seeds.
preparation: Check ripeness — gives slightly when pressed.
Cut in half and scoop flesh with a spoon.
Or peel the skin and slice. The small black seeds are edible.
plants.nutrition: Extremely high in Vitamin C, also Vitamin K and fibre.
warnings: Can cause allergic reactions in some people.

plants.identification: Oval yellow fruit with a pointed tip, rough waxy skin.
preparation: Use juice — squeeze over food or mix with water.
Zest the skin for flavouring.
Mix juice with water and sugar for a safe energy drink in survival.
plants.nutrition: Very high in Vitamin C and citric acid.
warnings: Undiluted lemon juice can damage tooth enamel.

plants.identification: Round fruit with rough pink-red bumpy skin, white translucent flesh, large brown seed.
preparation: Pick only fully red or pink ripe lychees.
Peel back the skin — it comes off easily when ripe.
Eat the white flesh. Do not eat the seed.
Do not eat unripe green lychees.
plants.nutrition: High in Vitamin C and copper.
warnings: Unripe lychees contain hypoglycin A which can cause dangerous low blood sugar.
Never eat on an empty stomach in large quantities.

plants.identification: Fleshy ovate fruit turning yellow or red when ripe. Leathery green leaves.
preparation: Check ripeness — soft to touch with sweet smell.
Wash the outer skin thoroughly.
Peel skin or slice flesh away from the large flat central seed.
Consume the fleshy yellow interior.
plants.nutrition: High in Vitamin C, carbohydrates, and natural sugars.
warnings: Sap from stem and peel can cause skin irritation in sensitive individuals.
Do not eat the seed.

plants.identification: Large oval fruit with tough spiky brown-green skin and a crown of stiff leaves.
preparation: Twist or cut off the crown.
Slice off the top and bottom, then cut away the outer skin.
Remove any remaining eyes with a knife.
Slice or chunk the yellow flesh.
plants.nutrition: High in Vitamin C, manganese, and bromelain enzyme.
warnings: Unripe pineapple can cause stomach upset.

plants.identification: Small to medium oval fruit, purple, red or yellow skin, juicy flesh.
preparation: Pick fully coloured plums that give slightly when pressed.
Wash and eat fresh — bite around the stone.
Do not eat the stone (seed).
Can be cooked into jam or dried into prunes.
plants.nutrition: Contains Vitamin C, K, and antioxidants.
warnings: Plum stones contain cyanogenic compounds — never crack or eat the seed.

plants.identification: Round fruit with tough leathery red-orange skin and crown-like calyx at top.
preparation: Score the skin from top to bottom into quarters.
Break apart along the scored lines.
Pull out the bright red arils (seed pods) — these are what you eat.
The white pith is bitter — discard it.
plants.nutrition: Extremely high in antioxidants, Vitamin C and folate.
warnings: The white pith is very bitter and indigestible — eat only the arils.

plants.identification: Small red aggregate berries on thorny canes with white-backed leaves.
preparation: Pick berries that are fully red and come off the cane easily.
Rinse gently with clean water.
Eat raw immediately — they do not keep long.
Cook into jam or boil for juice.
plants.nutrition: Very high in fibre, Vitamin C, and antioxidants.
warnings: Do not confuse with unripe blackberries — true raspberries are hollow when picked.

plants.identification: Small red heart-shaped fruit with tiny seeds on surface and green leafy cap.
preparation: Pick fully red strawberries.
Remove the green cap and leaves.
Rinse and eat raw — wild strawberries are smaller but sweeter.
Can be mashed for flavouring or cooked into jam.
plants.nutrition: High in Vitamin C, manganese, and folate.
warnings: Rarely cause allergic reactions in sensitive individuals.

plants.identification: Large round or oval fruit with green striped skin, bright red or pink flesh with black seeds.
preparation: Thump the watermelon — a hollow sound means it is ripe.
Cut in half with a large knife.
Slice into wedges and eat the red flesh.
Seeds are edible — can be roasted as a snack.
plants.nutrition: 92% water, Vitamins A, B6 and C, and lycopene.
warnings: Eating very large amounts can cause digestive upset.

plants.identification: Large star-shaped leaves, spiny seed pods, mottled brown-white oval seeds.
plants.nutrition: None — extremely toxic.
warnings: Contains ricin — one of the most toxic natural substances known.
Even 1-2 seeds can be fatal to a child.
Do not touch seed pods — wash hands immediately if contact occurs.
plants.whyToxic: Seeds contain ricin, a protein synthesis inhibitor that causes cell death throughout the body.
plants.emergency: Call emergency services immediately. Do not induce vomiting. Go to hospital — there is no antidote, only supportive treatment.

plants.identification: Mango-like fruit, green turning red. White star-shaped flowers with sweet scent.
plants.nutrition: None — extremely toxic.
warnings: All parts of the plant are toxic.
The fruit resembles a small mango — do not be deceived.
Do not burn the wood — smoke is toxic.
plants.whyToxic: Contains cerberin and cerberoside — cardiac glycosides that stop the heart.
plants.emergency: Call emergency services immediately. This is a cardiac emergency — rush to hospital.

plants.identification: Large trumpet-shaped white or purple flowers, spiny green seed pods, large toothed leaves.
plants.nutrition: None — highly toxic.
warnings: All parts of the plant are toxic — especially seeds.
Do not touch eyes after handling.
plants.whyToxic: Contains tropane alkaloids (atropine, scopolamine) causing severe anticholinergic toxicity.
plants.emergency: Call emergency services immediately. Keep person calm. Do not induce vomiting.

plants.identification: Shiny black berries, dull green leaves, bell-shaped purple-brown flowers.
plants.nutrition: None — extremely toxic.
warnings: The berries look attractive — 2-5 berries can kill a child.
All parts of the plant are toxic.
Wear gloves if you must handle this plant.
plants.whyToxic: Contains atropine and scopolamine — blocks acetylcholine causing complete nervous system failure.
plants.emergency: Call emergency services immediately. This is a life-threatening emergency. Do not induce vomiting.

plants.identification: Small round berries, green turning orange-red. Looks similar to cherry tomato.
plants.nutrition: None — toxic.
warnings: Looks exactly like a cherry tomato — it is NOT edible.
Commonly grown as ornamental plant — keep away from children.
plants.whyToxic: Contains solanine and solanocapsine causing gastrointestinal and nervous system toxicity.
plants.emergency: Call Poison Control. If large amount consumed, go to hospital immediately.

plants.identification: Small round berries, green turning black-purple in clusters. Colourful flower clusters nearby.
plants.nutrition: None — toxic.
warnings: Unripe green berries are more toxic than ripe dark ones — both are dangerous.
One of the most common causes of plant poisoning in children.
plants.whyToxic: Contains lantadene A and B causing liver damage and nervous system effects.
plants.emergency: Call Poison Control or go to hospital. Monitor for liver symptoms over next 48 hours.

plants.identification: Large plant with dark purple-black berries in grape-like clusters, thick purple-red stems.
plants.nutrition: None — toxic raw.
warnings: All parts are toxic — especially roots and seeds.
Berries look like grapes — do not confuse.
Even handling can cause skin irritation.
plants.whyToxic: Contains phytolaccatoxin causing severe gastrointestinal and nervous system toxicity.
plants.emergency: Call emergency services. Wash skin if contacted. Do not induce vomiting.

plants.identification: Bright red seeds with a black spot at one end. Vine with small pink-purple flowers.
plants.nutrition: None — extremely toxic.
warnings: A single chewed seed can be fatal.
Seeds are used in jewellery — puncturing the seed releases toxin.
plants.whyToxic: Contains abrin — similar to ricin, inhibits protein synthesis causing cell death.
plants.emergency: Call emergency services immediately. Rush to hospital — life-threatening emergency.

plants.identification: Large tree with spiky trunk, pumpkin-shaped seed pods that explode when dry.
plants.nutrition: None — toxic.
warnings: Exploding seed pods can hurl seeds at 70 mph causing injury.
Sap causes severe skin burns and blindness.
Do not shelter under this tree or burn its wood.
plants.whyToxic: Contains hurin and crepitin — phorbol esters causing severe inflammation and toxic effects.
symptoms: Eye damage or blindness from sap
plants.emergency: Rinse skin and eyes immediately with large amounts of water. Seek medical attention urgently.

plants.identification: Bright red cup-shaped berry with a visible dark seed inside. Dark evergreen needles.
plants.nutrition: None — extremely toxic.
warnings: The red flesh is not toxic but the seed inside IS — do not eat.
All other parts (bark, needles, seeds) are extremely toxic.
Death can occur within hours of ingestion.
plants.whyToxic: Contains taxine alkaloids which stop the heart within hours.
plants.emergency: Call emergency services immediately. Cardiac monitoring required. Rush to hospital — no antidote exists.
